This seems confusing.   I see that the lspi code (in dlib/test/lspi.cpp) still defines feature extractors this way.  So this is wrong isn't it?  This function still needs to be here.

A better way to organize this model/feature extractor stuff is probably to have the documentation say there is just one class, not two.  Then you can define two different versions of the model class.  There is the basic one which was previously defined by the example_feature_extractor.  Then there is the more detailed one with the new things you want to add to models.  The new one has all the functions the feature extractor had, but with some additional new ones.  Then when you use any model class it's not like it depends on some feature extractor class.  It's just one class.

After all, the model is the thing the user makes.  So it's weird that later on it's defined to take a feature extractor as a template argument.

Anyway, my point is that it's really unclear what a model or feature extractor is right now.  Clarifying that is the most important thing here since that's the interface a user of the software is expected to implement.  If it's not really clear people will just not use it and this whole RL module will never be used.  Making that model interface simple so people can easily implement it is the central challenge.

I've thought a lot about what to do with style problems here where assert is a statement but you've got a really long line. Generally, I want the end result to be easily parsed into "this == that". My current style is to do something like this:

```
assert (
    get_keys_from_envs(reversed([{'L': 'v'}, {'L': 'w'},]), '1') ==
    'w'
)
```

That form works pretty well for long lines because it puts the expression far to the left plus it breaks up the "this" and "that" into separate lines. If the "this" expression got even longer, then you get to just continue using normal indenting rules.

To clarify, I'm not advocating we change this. It's just something I think about periodically and figured I'd share.

`y[x]` is advanced indexing which is usually slower than other indexing operations due to two reasons.
1. Overhead of sanity checking and preprocessing advanced indices before calling backend ops.
2. In this case, the backend op used is `gather_nd`, which is expected for retrieving scattered elements from an ndarray. For a regular shape index like `[1, 3]` indexing the first dimension, operators such as `take` or `slice` are much more efficient than `gather_nd`.

> Determine on base of WER, if model starts overfitting

I don't think this makes sense. 

I read this as saying: If the model starts overfitting, then stop training only once the validation WER is below the target threshold.

Overfitting can occur and the validation WER could be above the threshold set and it can stay there. So, this could lead to an infinite loop.

We don't have to do this now, but we could use early stopping using the ValidationMonitor[[1]](https://www.tensorflow.org/versions/r0.10/tutorials/monitors/index.html#early-stopping-with-validationmonitor) and base early stopping on WER.

So trying this removal reminded me why I put it in. In edge cases when you are logged in, and log out, the save menu states are stale and don't reflect you are logged out. It appears you need `user.loggedin` in the markup for Save menus to behave correctly and appear disabled in appropriate conditions. I'd like to leave this in on that basis, unless there's some solution I haven't figured out :)

Easy way to reproduce this is to remove the 4 occurrences of `!user.loggedin` tests here above where `canSaveClassifications()` exsists in the markup, login under vagrant, pin a job, select a non-default classification from the dropdown, then log out. I wanted to provide a little background here about dependencies in Certbot. First, I'll say that too many dependencies is one of the most common complaints we have. Some people have small lightweight servers and hate having to install 35+ packages just to get a cert. This can be mitigated by making Certbot more modular but this increases the complexity for people getting the pieces of Certbot they need for their use case. It's a trade off and we should try to strike a reasonable balance here.

Additionally, our current approach to keeping Certbot reasonably up to date as we and the ACME protocol continue to change rapidly is to try and convince Linux distros to update us more quickly than they usually do. This usually involves a lot of compromise and one instance of this is we told Debian we wouldn't add any non-optional dependencies to Certbot. None of these things are total blockers to us adding dependencies and there are definite benefits to doing so but I wanted to bring them up so we can make the right design decision here.

One question I had is about the viability of using the [lexicon](https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon) project you found for many of our DNS plugins including this one. All of the DNS plugins that have been implemented so far are supposedly supported by lexicon with the exception of Google Cloud DNS which they claim to be open to accepting a PR for. If we go this route, I'd also be curious if restructuring our DNS plugins makes sense if there's a common base and level of abstraction from lexicon we can use to easily support all/many of these DNS providers.

If for some reason this doesn't work, I think we have two options for this plugin. We could do both of these options but I think at least one is necessary.

1. We split it out into its own package.
2. We vendorize the dependencies that aren't widely packaged by OSes such as `cloudflare` and `logger`. I haven't looked into exactly what we'd need to vendorize here but I'm happy to do so if we go this route.

Not doing one of these two things means not only did we go back on our promise to Debian, but we now depend on libraries that aren't packaged. We can talk to our package maintainer if we really want to go this route, but I think we'll have a bad time.

The scenario: you click a "Deploy with Pulumi" button on a README.md, which would take you to a landing page in the Pulumi Console that lets you create a new stack and fill out the config on that page. That page would be responsible for creating the stack, populating it with an initial empty deployment (which just contains the config that was filled out on the page) and then provides the user with the `pulumi up <url> -s <stack>` command to copy/paste to run. When that command runs, we need the CLI to see that the stack already exists, has an empty deployment that contains config that satisfies the template's config requirements, and contains a `pulumi:template` config value containing a matching URL. In which case we can just use that config, without prompting from the CLI.

I need to fix the code to check to make sure the URL specified on the command line matches the URL stored in the `pulumi:template` config value, and that the rest of the config from the stack satisfies the config requirements of the template. Right now it's just blindly accepting it.

Yeah, I played around with it in the console and I see what's going on. The `src` attribute is a string that points to the location of the resource, which in this case is an in-memory blob.

I think the only downside of this `startsWith('blob:')` approach is that a user might create *another* blob that they *don't* want revoked, and pass that one's resource locator to the model, and when they change the image, we'll be freeing their blob, not *our* blob. This seems like an extreme edge case, though, and I'm sure there are simple enough workarounds that dealing with this edge case is not worth adding additional complexity.

I imagine that the "right" way to do this (which avoids this edge case) would be to set a flag on the model that indicates whether the current URL was allocated by `update`, and if so free it - rather than trying to infer this from the value at all.
